title: What belongs here
summary: Board games, digital card games, tactics games, auto-resolved conflict systems, and other games where the core state progression happens through discrete validated moves.
content:
Board games, digital card games, tactics games, auto-resolved conflict systems, and
other games where the core state progression happens through discrete validated moves.

title: Core structural rule
summary: Treat the game as a state machine with explicit moves, turn order, and phase changes. If the game cannot be replayed from a sequence of inputs, the architecture is probably too ent
content:
Treat the game as a state machine with explicit moves, turn order, and phase changes.
If the game cannot be replayed from a sequence of inputs, the architecture is probably
too entangled.

title: Preferred project structure
summary: - core rules engine - state schema and serialization layer - move validation and reducers - phase and turn-order system - client presentation - AI or simulation harness - logs, rep
content:
- core rules engine
- state schema and serialization layer
- move validation and reducers
- phase and turn-order system
- client presentation
- AI or simulation harness
- logs, replay, and debugging tools

title: Source-of-truth boundaries
summary: The source of truth should usually be: - state schema - move definitions - effect resolution rules - setup and phase definitions - card, unit, or board data Rendering should not ow
content:
The source of truth should usually be:

- state schema
- move definitions
- effect resolution rules
- setup and phase definitions
- card, unit, or board data

Rendering should not own rules. UI convenience caches are allowed, but the authoritative
outcome should come from the rules engine.

title: Verification model
summary: This family should be the easiest to test exhaustively. Temper should encourage: - deterministic test seeds - replay from move logs - snapshotting of state transitions - simulation
content:
This family should be the easiest to test exhaustively.

Temper should encourage:

- deterministic test seeds
- replay from move logs
- snapshotting of state transitions
- simulation against dominant strategies or edge-case loops

title: Release and hotfix concerns
summary: Hotfixes are safer when the rules engine is isolated. The biggest risk is not usually deploy mechanics but invalidating live matches, migrations, or existing saves. Before shipping
content:
Hotfixes are safer when the rules engine is isolated. The biggest risk is not usually
deploy mechanics but invalidating live matches, migrations, or existing saves.

Before shipping:

- verify move legality and resolution
- verify save and replay compatibility
- verify content data changes against balance assumptions

title: Failure modes
summary: - hidden state living only in UI or network code - nondeterminism entering move resolution - ambiguous timing windows - incomplete logs that make replays untrustworthy - content pa
content:
- hidden state living only in UI or network code
- nondeterminism entering move resolution
- ambiguous timing windows
- incomplete logs that make replays untrustworthy
- content patches that change rules without versioning old matches or saves

title: Team operating notes
summary: This family works especially well with multi-session development if the rules engine is clean and the logs are stable. Multiple contributors can safely work in parallel when they o
content:
This family works especially well with multi-session development if the rules engine is
clean and the logs are stable. Multiple contributors can safely work in parallel when
they own different cards, effects, AI, or UI layers, as long as move semantics remain
centralized.

title: Best Temper overlays
summary: - `Meier ♟️` for choice quality - `Kaplan 🎯` for fairness and clarity - `Carmack 🔧` for determinism and debugging
content:
- `Meier ♟️` for choice quality
- `Kaplan 🎯` for fairness and clarity
- `Carmack 🔧` for determinism and debugging
